title: PostgreSQL High Availability: Complete Guide with Patroni, ETCD & Multi-Port Setup
description: A comprehensive, step-by-step guide to building a production-grade PostgreSQL High Availability cluster using Patroni and ETCD. Learn to deploy multiple PostgreSQL instances on different ports, configure automatic failover, set up HAProxy load balancing, and run multiple HA clusters side by side with zero downtime.
author: E-Learning Platform
heroAlt: PostgreSQL High Availability architecture diagram showing 3-node Patroni cluster with ETCD, HAProxy, and application layer

sections.title: Introduction
content: Database downtime costs businesses millions every year. Whether you're running an e-commerce platform, a SaaS application, or a critical enterprise system, losing your database means losing customers, revenue, and trust. PostgreSQL High Availability (HA) solves this problem by ensuring your database remains accessible even when individual servers fail.

In this comprehensive guide, you'll learn how to build a production-grade PostgreSQL HA cluster using Patroni (the industry-standard HA manager) and ETCD (a distributed key-value store for leader election). We'll cover everything from architecture design to hands-on configuration, including running multiple PostgreSQL instances on different ports simultaneously.
By the end of this guide, you'll have a fully automated, self-healing PostgreSQL cluster that can survive server failures without manual intervention.

infoBox.content: This guide uses PostgreSQL 16, Patroni 4.x, ETCD 3.5, and HAProxy 2.x. All commands are tested on Ubuntu 22.04 LTS but work on Debian, RHEL, Rocky Linux, and CentOS with minimal adjustments.

sections.title: Architecture Overview
content: Before we dive into the setup, let's understand how the pieces fit together.

cards.title: PostgreSQL
cards.desc: The world's most advanced open-source relational database. Provides streaming replication for keeping replicas in sync with the primary.

cards.title: Patroni
cards.desc: A Python-based HA template for PostgreSQL. Automates leader election, replica management, and handles failover when the primary fails.

cards.title: ETCD
cards.desc: A distributed key-value store that acts as the source of truth. Stores cluster state, leader information, and coordinates decisions between Patroni nodes.

cards.title: HAProxy
cards.desc: A high-performance TCP/HTTP load balancer. Routes read/write traffic to the appropriate PostgreSQL node based on Patroni's REST API health checks.

sections.title: Architecture Diagram
content: The following architecture shows a 3-node PostgreSQL cluster managed by Patroni, with ETCD providing distributed consensus and HAProxy routing application traffic.

image.alt: PostgreSQL HA architecture with 3 nodes, ETCD cluster, HAProxy, and application layer

subsections.title: Data Flow Summary
subsections.content: Applications connect to HAProxy ports 5000 (write) and 5001 (read). HAProxy forwards writes to the Patroni leader and distributes reads across all replicas. Patroni monitors PostgreSQL health via its REST API and triggers automatic failover if the leader fails.

sections.title: Prerequisites
content: You need at least 3 Linux servers (physical or virtual) to build a high-availability cluster. A 3-node setup gives you reliable failover and tolerates the loss of any single node.

rows: Low latency is critical for replication

items.label: Hostname Configuration
items.text: Use the following hostnames and IP addresses throughout this guide. Adjust based on your actual network.

items.label: /etc/hosts entries (all nodes)
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/hosts

# Add these lines to all 3 nodes
192.168.1.201  node1.local  node1
192.168.1.202  node2.local  node2
192.168.1.203  node3.local  node3

items.label: Hostname setup (run on each node)
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node1
root@node2:~$ sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node2
root@node3:~$ sudo hostnamectl set-hostname node3

sections.title: Part 1: ETCD Cluster Setup
content: ETCD is the distributed key-value store that Patroni uses as its Distributed Configuration Store (DCS). It's responsible for leader election, storing cluster state, and coordinating decisions between Patroni nodes. We'll install a 3-node ETCD cluster for high availability.
ETCD uses port 2379 for client communication and port 2380 for peer-to-peer communication between ETCD nodes.

subsections.title: Step 1: Install ETCD on All Nodes
subsections.description: Install ETCD using the official script from the ETCD project.

items.label: Download and install ETCD
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get update
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install etcd -y

# If not available in repos, download manually:
root@node1:~$ ETCD_VER=v3.5.15
root@node1:~$ wget https://github.com/etcd-io/etcd/releases/download/${ETCD_VER}/etcd-${ETCD_VER}-linux-amd64.tar.gz
root@node1:~$ tar xzf etcd-${ETCD_VER}-linux-amd64.tar.gz
root@node1:~$ sudo mv etcd-${ETCD_VER}-linux-amd64/{etcd,etcdctl} /usr/local/bin/
root@node1:~$ rm -rf etcd-${ETCD_VER}-linux-amd64*

# Verify installation
root@node1:~$ etcd --version
etcd Version: 3.5.15
Go Version: go1.21.8
Git SHA: a2e0ef46a

subsections.title: Step 2: Configure ETCD on Node 1
subsections.description: Create the ETCD configuration file on the first node.

items.label: ETCD configuration node1
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/etcd /var/lib/etcd
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml

# Paste the following:
name: 'node1'
data-dir: '/var/lib/etcd'
listen-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.201:2380'
listen-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.201:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379'
advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.201:2380'
advertise-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.201:2379'
initial-advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.201:2380'
initial-cluster: 'node1=http://192.168.1.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.1.202:2380,node3=http://192.168.1.203:2380'
initial-cluster-token: 'pg-ha-etcd'
initial-cluster-state: 'new'

items.label: ETCD configuration node2
items.code:
root@node2:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/etcd /var/lib/etcd
root@node2:~$ sudo nano /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml

name: 'node2'
data-dir: '/var/lib/etcd'
listen-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.202:2380'
listen-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.202:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379'
advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.202:2380'
advertise-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.202:2379'
initial-advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.202:2380'
initial-cluster: 'node1=http://192.168.1.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.1.202:2380,node3=http://192.168.1.203:2380'
initial-cluster-token: 'pg-ha-etcd'
initial-cluster-state: 'new'

items.label: ETCD configuration node3
items.code:
root@node3:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/etcd /var/lib/etcd
root@node3:~$ sudo nano /etc/etcd/etcd.conf.yml

name: 'node3'
data-dir: '/var/lib/etcd'
listen-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.203:2380'
listen-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.203:2379,http://127.0.0.1:2379'
advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.203:2380'
advertise-client-urls: 'http://192.168.1.203:2379'
initial-advertise-peer-urls: 'http://192.168.1.203:2380'
initial-cluster: 'node1=http://192.168.1.201:2380,node2=http://192.168.1.202:2380,node3=http://192.168.1.203:2380'
initial-cluster-token: 'pg-ha-etcd'
initial-cluster-state: 'new'

items.label: Start ETCD on all nodes
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start etcd
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl enable etcd

root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl start etcd
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl enable etcd

root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl start etcd
root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl enable etcd

sections.title: Part 2: PostgreSQL Installation on All Nodes
content: Install PostgreSQL 16 on all three nodes. We'll install it from the official PostgreSQL apt repository for the latest stable version.

items.label: Install on all nodes
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install -y postgresql-common
root@node1:~$ sudo sh /usr/share/postgresql-common/pgdg/apt.postgresql.org.sh

# Install PostgreSQL 16
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install postgresql-16 postgresql-client-16 -y

# Verify installation
root@node1:~$ psql --version
psql (PostgreSQL) 16.4
root@node1:~$ pg_config --version
PostgreSQL 16.4

subsections.title: Create PostgreSQL Data Directories for Multi-Port Setup
subsections.content: Since we plan to run multiple PostgreSQL instances on different ports, create separate data directories for each instance. This is critical for the multi-database, multi-port setup.

items.label: Create data directories for 3 instances
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/{cluster1,cluster2,cluster3}
root@node1:~$ sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /data/pg
root@node1:~$ sudo chmod 700 /data/pg/*

# Verify
root@node1:~$ ls -la /data/pg/
total 20
drwx------ 3 postgres postgres 4096 May 19 10:00 cluster1
drwx------ 3 postgres postgres 4096 May 19 10:00 cluster2
drwx------ 3 postgres postgres 4096 May 19 10:00 cluster3

sections.title: Part 3: Patroni Installation
content: Patroni is a Python application. We'll install it in a virtual environment to keep dependencies isolated.

items.label: Install dependencies and Patroni
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install python3 python3-pip python3-venv -y

# Install psycopg2 binary (PostgreSQL adapter)
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install libpq-dev -y
root@node1:~$ sudo pip3 install psycopg2-binary

# Install Patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]

# Verify
root@node1:~$ patroni --version
patroni 4.1.3

root@node2:~$ sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]
root@node3:~$ sudo pip3 install patroni[etcd3]

items.label: Create Patroni system user
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /bin/false patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo usermod -aG postgres patroni

sections.title: Part 4: Patroni Configuration for 3-Node Cluster on Different Ports
content: Now we configure Patroni to manage PostgreSQL instances on different ports across our 3-node cluster. Each node will have a unique PostgreSQL port but share the same cluster configuration via ETCD.

subsections.title: Patroni Configuration File Structure
subsections.content: Each Patroni node needs a YAML configuration file. The configuration tells Patroni how to connect to ETCD, which PostgreSQL instance to manage, on which port to run its REST API, and what replication settings to use.

items.label: Create config directory
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo chown -R patroni:patroni /etc/patroni

subsections.title: Node 1: Leader Candidate (Port 5432)
subsections.content: This node will be the initial leader (primary). It runs PostgreSQL on port 5432.

items.label: Node 1 Patroni configuration
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# /etc/patroni/patroni.yml - Node 1
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node1-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.201:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.1.201:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        wal_level: replica
        wal_log_hints: 'on'
        max_wal_senders: 10
        max_replication_slots: 10
        wal_keep_size: 512
        max_connections: 200
        shared_buffers: 1GB
        work_mem: 128MB
        maintenance_work_mem: 256MB
        effective_cache_size: 3GB

  initdb:
  - encoding: UTF8
  - locale: en_US.UTF-8
  - data-checksums

  pg_hba:
  - host replication replicator 192.168.1.0/24 trust
  - host all all 192.168.1.0/24 trust
  - host all all 127.0.0.1/32 trust

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.201:5432
  connect_address: 192.168.1.201:5432
  data_dir: /data/patroni/pg
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5432

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Node 2: Replica (Port 5433)
subsections.content: This node runs as a replica on port 5433.

items.label: Node 2 Patroni configuration
items.code:
root@node2:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
root@node2:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# /etc/patroni/patroni.yml - Node 2
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node2-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.202:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.1.202:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.202:5433
  connect_address: 192.168.1.202:5433
  data_dir: /data/patroni/pg
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5433

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Node 3: Replica (Port 5434)
subsections.content: This node runs as a replica on port 5434.

items.label: Node 3 Patroni configuration
items.code:
root@node3:~$ sudo mkdir -p /etc/patroni
root@node3:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni.yml

# /etc/patroni/patroni.yml - Node 3
scope: pg-ha-cluster
namespace: /db/
name: node3-pg

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.203:8008
  connect_address: 192.168.1.203:8008
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123
  https: false

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.203:5434
  connect_address: 192.168.1.203:5434
  data_dir: /data/patroni/pg
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator
      password: repl-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user
      password: rewind-pass-000
  parameters:
    unix_socket_directories: '/var/run/postgresql'
    port: 5434

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

rows: Maximum retry timeout for DCS operations
Max replication lag before promoting replica
Allow former leader to rejoin as replica
Use replication slots to prevent WAL loss

sections.title: Part 5: Starting the Patroni Cluster
content: With all configurations in place, it's time to bootstrap and start the cluster. The first node started will initialize the PostgreSQL data directory and become the leader.

subsections.title: Stop Existing PostgreSQL Service
subsections.content: If the default PostgreSQL service is running, stop and disable it to avoid port conflicts.

items.label: Stop system PostgreSQL on all nodes
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop postgresql
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl disable postgresql
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl mask postgresql

root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl stop postgresql
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl disable postgresql
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl mask postgresql

root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl stop postgresql
root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl disable postgresql
root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl mask postgresql

subsections.title: Start Node 1 First (Initial Leader)
subsections.content: The first node initializes the PostgreSQL data directory and creates the initial cluster state in ETCD.

items.label: Create data directory and start Patroni
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /data/patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo chown patroni:postgres /data/patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo chmod 775 /data/patroni

# Start Patroni manually to check logs
root@node1:~$ sudo -u patroni patroni /etc/patroni/patroni.yml &

# Check logs
root@node1:~$ sudo journalctl -u patroni -f
# Or check the Patroni log directly
root@node1:~$ sudo -u patroni tail -f /var/log/patroni.log

subsections.title: Create Systemd Service for Patroni (All Nodes)
subsections.content: Create a systemd service so Patroni runs automatically on boot.

items.label: Systemd service file
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/patroni.service

[Unit]
Description=Patroni: PostgreSQL High Availability
After=etcd.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=patroni
Group=patroni
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=10
TimeoutSec=30

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

items.label: Enable and start Patroni service
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni

# Verify
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl status patroni
Patroni: PostgreSQL High Availability
  Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/patroni.service; enabled)
  Active: active (running) since ...

# Create same service on Node 2 and Node 3
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni

root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni
root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni

items.label: Check via Patroni REST API
items.code:
root@node1:~$ curl -s http://192.168.1.201:8008/health | python3 -m json.tool
{
    "state": "running",
    "postmaster_start_time": "2026-05-19 10:30:00 UTC",
    "role": "primary",
    "server_version": 160004,
    "timeline": 1
}

root@node1:~$ curl -s http://192.168.1.202:8008/health | python3 -m json.tool
{
    "state": "running",
    "postmaster_start_time": "2026-05-19 10:30:15 UTC",
    "role": "replica",
    "server_version": 160004,
    "timeline": 1
}

sections.title: Part 6: Working with patronictl
content: patronictl is the command-line tool for managing and monitoring your Patroni cluster. Learn the essential commands.

items.label: Manual switchover
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml switchover --master node1-pg --candidate node2-pg --scheduled now
Current cluster topology
+ Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member    | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-pg  | 192.168.1.201:5432| Leader  | running | 1 |   0 |
| node2-pg  | 192.168.1.202:5433| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
| node3-pg  | 192.168.1.203:5434| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
Are you sure you want to switchover cluster pg-ha-cluster? [y/N]: y
Switchover successful

# Verify new leader
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
+ Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member    | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-pg  | 192.168.1.201:5432| Replica | running | 2 |   0 |
| node2-pg  | 192.168.1.202:5433| Leader  | running | 2 |   0 |
| node3-pg  | 192.168.1.203:5434| Replica | running | 2 |   0 |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+

items.label: View cluster history
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml history

+ Timeline | LSN    | Leader         | Reason +
+----------+--------+----------------+-------+
|        1 | 0/90000| node1-pg       |       |
|        2 | 0/A0000| node2-pg       | switchover |
+----------+--------+----------------+-------+

# Get cluster configuration
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml show-config

ttl: 30
loop_wait: 10
retry_timeout: 10
maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
postgresql:
  use_pg_rewind: true
  use_slots: true
  parameters:
    wal_level: replica
    wal_log_hints: 'on'

items.label: Reinitialize a failed replica
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml reinit pg-ha-cluster node3-pg
Are you sure you want to reinitialize node3-pg? [y/N]: y
Reinitializing node3-pg...

sections.title: Part 7: HAProxy Load Balancer Setup
content: HAProxy routes application traffic to the correct PostgreSQL node. Write queries go to the leader, read queries can be distributed across all replicas. HAProxy uses Patroni's REST API to dynamically detect which node is the leader.

items.label: Install on all nodes (or a dedicated load balancer)
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install haproxy -y
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop haproxy

subsections.title: Configure HAProxy
subsections.content: Create the HAProxy configuration that uses Patroni's REST API to dynamically route traffic.

items.label: HAProxy configuration
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg

global
    maxconn 1000
    log 127.0.0.1 local2
    stats socket /var/run/haproxy.sock mode 600 level admin
    stats timeout 30s

defaults
    mode tcp
    log global
    option tcplog
    timeout connect 5000ms
    timeout client 50000ms
    timeout server 50000ms
    timeout check 2000ms

# HAProxy Statistics Dashboard
listen stats
    mode http
    bind *:7000
    stats enable
    stats uri /stats
    stats refresh 10s
    stats auth admin:admin-pass-123

# PostgreSQL Write (Primary) - Port 5000
listen pg-write
    bind *:5000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    
    server node1-pg 192.168.1.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.1.202:5433 check port 8008
    server node3-pg 192.168.1.203:5434 check port 8008

# PostgreSQL Read (Replicas) - Port 5001
listen pg-read
    bind *:5001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2
    
    server node1-pg 192.168.1.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.1.202:5433 check port 8008
    server node3-pg 192.168.1.203:5434 check port 8008

rows: Write queries → always routed to the primary/leader
Read queries → load balanced across replicas

items.label: Health Check Logic
text: HAProxy checks Patroni's REST API on port 8008 every 3 seconds:
• GET /primary → returns HTTP 200 if this node is the leader
• GET /replica → returns HTTP 200 if this node is a replica
If a check fails 3 times, the server is marked as down.
When the leader fails, HAProxy automatically detects the new leader via Patroni's REST API.

items.label: Enable and start HAProxy
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl enable haproxy
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start haproxy

# Verify
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl status haproxy
HAProxy Load Balancer
  Loaded: loaded
  Active: active (running)

# Check HAProxy stats
root@node1:~$ curl -u admin:admin-pass-123 http://localhost:7000/stats

items.label: Connect to PostgreSQL through HAProxy
items.code:
root@node1:~$ psql -h 192.168.1.201 -p 5000 -U postgres -d postgres
Password for user postgres:
postgres=# SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port();
 inet_server_addr | inet_server_port
------------------+------------------
 192.168.1.201    |            5432
(1 row)

# This confirms we reached the leader through port 5000

postgres=# CREATE DATABASE testdb;
CREATE DATABASE
postgres=# \q

# Test read connection
root@node1:~$ psql -h 192.168.1.201 -p 5001 -U postgres -d postgres
postgres=# SELECT inet_server_addr(), inet_server_port();
 inet_server_addr | inet_server_port
------------------+------------------
 192.168.1.202    |            5433
(1 row)

sections.title: Part 8: Testing High Availability (Failover)
content: The most important test of any HA system is simulating a failure and verifying automatic recovery.

items.label: Test 1: Stop the Leader Process
items.code:
# Simulate leader failure by stopping Patroni (which stops PostgreSQL)
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop patroni

# On another node, check the cluster
root@node2:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
+ Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member    | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-pg  | 192.168.1.201:5432| unknown | stopped | 1 |   0 |
| node2-pg  | 192.168.1.202:5433| Leader  | running | 2 |   0 |
| node3-pg  | 192.168.1.203:5434| Replica | running | 2 |   0 |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+

# Expected: Node 2 was automatically promoted to leader!
# Node 1 is now stopped/down

items.label: Test 2: Bring the Failed Node Back
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni

# Check cluster - node1 rejoins as replica
root@node2:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
+ Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member    | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-pg  | 192.168.1.201:5432| Replica | running | 2 |   0 |
| node2-pg  | 192.168.1.202:5433| Leader  | running | 2 |   0 |
| node3-pg  | 192.168.1.203:5434| Replica | running | 2 |   0 |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+

# Node 1 automatically rejoined as a replica!
# Timeline 2 means a new leader timeline was created

items.label: Test 3: Kill PostgreSQL Directly
items.code:
# Kill PostgreSQL process on the leader node
root@node2:~$ sudo kill -9 $(head -n 1 /data/patroni/pg/postmaster.pid)

# Patroni detects the crash and restarts PostgreSQL
# Check patroni logs:
root@node2:~$ sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 20 --no-pager

# Wait ~10 seconds for loop_wait
# Check cluster status
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

# Expected: Patroni restarted PostgreSQL on node2
# If it fails to restart after retry_timeout, node2 is marked as failed
# Another replica is promoted to leader

items.label: Test 4: Network Partition
items.code:
# Simulate network failure (block ETCD port)
root@node2:~$ sudo iptables -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 2379 -j DROP

# Patroni loses connection to ETCD after ttl (30 seconds)
# The leader cannot renew its lock in ETCD -> replicas notice
# After ttl expires, replicas hold a new leader election

# Restore connectivity
root@node2:~$ sudo iptables -D INPUT -p tcp --dport 2379 -j DROP

# Check cluster
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list

sections.title: Part 9: Running Multiple HA Clusters on Different Ports
content: In production, you often need to run multiple PostgreSQL clusters side by side. Perhaps you have different applications, environments (staging, production), or tenants. Patroni makes this straightforward by using different scopes and ports for each cluster.
Each cluster needs its own unique scope name in ETCD, its own set of PostgreSQL ports, and its own Patroni configuration file.

subsections.title: Cluster Layout: Two Separate HA Clusters
subsections.content: We'll create two independent HA clusters on the same 3 nodes:

subsections.title: Configuration for Cluster A (already done above)
subsections.content: Cluster A uses scope 'pg-ha-cluster' with ports 5432, 5433, 5434 as configured in Part 4.

items.label: Cluster B - Node 1 Patroni config
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

# /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml
scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node1-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.201:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.1.201:8009
  authentication:
    username: patroni_api
    password: secure-api-pass-123

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

bootstrap:
  dcs:
    ttl: 30
    loop_wait: 10
    retry_timeout: 10
    maximum_lag_on_failover: 1048576
    postgresql:
      use_pg_rewind: true
      use_slots: true
      parameters:
        wal_level: replica
        wal_log_hints: 'on'
        max_wal_senders: 10
        max_replication_slots: 10
        wal_keep_size: 512
        max_connections: 100
        shared_buffers: 512MB

  initdb:
  - encoding: UTF8
  - locale: en_US.UTF-8
  - data-checksums

  pg_hba:
  - host replication replicator 192.168.1.0/24 trust
  - host all all 192.168.1.0/24 trust

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.201:5442
  connect_address: 192.168.1.201:5442
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node1
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  pgpass: /tmp/pgpass-cluster-b
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5442

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

items.label: Cluster B - Node 2 Patroni config
items.code:
root@node2:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node2-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.202:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.1.202:8009

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.202:5443
  connect_address: 192.168.1.202:5443
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node2
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5443

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

items.label: Cluster B - Node 3 Patroni config
items.code:
root@node3:~$ sudo nano /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml

scope: pg-cluster-b
namespace: /db/
name: node3-cluster-b

restapi:
  listen: 192.168.1.203:8009
  connect_address: 192.168.1.203:8009

etcd:
  hosts: 192.168.1.201:2379,192.168.1.202:2379,192.168.1.203:2379

postgresql:
  listen: 192.168.1.203:5444
  connect_address: 192.168.1.203:5444
  data_dir: /data/pg/cluster_b/node3
  bin_dir: /usr/lib/postgresql/16/bin
  authentication:
    replication:
      username: replicator_b
      password: repl-b-pass-456
    superuser:
      username: postgres
      password: super-b-pass-789
    rewind:
      username: rewind_user_b
      password: rewind-b-pass-000
  parameters:
    port: 5444

tags:
  nofailover: false
  noloadbalance: false
  clonefrom: false
  nosync: false

subsections.title: Create Data Directories for Cluster B

items.label: Create directories on all nodes
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node1
root@node1:~$ sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

root@node2:~$ sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node2
root@node2:~$ sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

root@node3:~$ sudo mkdir -p /data/pg/cluster_b/node3
root@node3:~$ sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/pg/cluster_b

items.label: Create systemd service for Cluster B
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/systemd/system/patroni-cluster-b.service

[Unit]
Description=Patroni Cluster B: PostgreSQL HA
After=etcd.service
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=patroni
Group=patroni
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/patroni /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml
KillMode=process
Restart=always
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

# Start on all nodes
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl daemon-reload
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni-cluster-b
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni-cluster-b

root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni-cluster-b
root@node2:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni-cluster-b

root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl enable patroni-cluster-b
root@node3:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni-cluster-b

items.label: List both clusters
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
+ Cluster: pg-ha-cluster ----------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member    | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-pg  | 192.168.1.201:5432| Leader  | running | 1 |   0 |
| node2-pg  | 192.168.1.202:5433| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
| node3-pg  | 192.168.1.203:5434| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
+-----------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+

root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni-cluster-b.yml list
+ Cluster: pg-cluster-b ------------+---------+----+-----------+
| Member         | Host              | Role    | State | TL | Lag |
+----------------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+
| node1-cluster-b| 192.168.1.201:5442| Leader  | running | 1 |   0 |
| node2-cluster-b| 192.168.1.202:5443| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
| node3-cluster-b| 192.168.1.203:5444| Replica | running | 1 |   0 |
+----------------+-------------------+---------+------+----+-----+

# Both clusters running independently!
# Each leader can fail independently
# Each cluster has its own replication stream

items.label: Verify different PostgreSQL ports
items.code:
root@node1:~$ netstat -tlnp | grep postgres
tcp  0  0 192.168.1.201:5432  0.0.0.0:*  LISTEN  12345/postgres
tcp  0  0 192.168.1.201:5442  0.0.0.0:*  LISTEN  12346/postgres

# Two PostgreSQL instances running on different ports!
# Independent failover, independent replication

items.label: HAProxy config with both clusters
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg

# Add to existing configuration:

# Cluster A Write
listen pg-cluster-a-write
    bind *:5000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server node1-pg 192.168.1.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.1.202:5433 check port 8008
    server node3-pg 192.168.1.203:5434 check port 8008

# Cluster A Read
listen pg-cluster-a-read
    bind *:5001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2
    server node1-pg 192.168.1.201:5432 check port 8008
    server node2-pg 192.168.1.202:5433 check port 8008
    server node3-pg 192.168.1.203:5434 check port 8008

# Cluster B Write
listen pg-cluster-b-write
    bind *:6000
    mode tcp
    option httpchk GET /primary
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 on-marked-down shutdown-sessions
    server node1-cluster-b 192.168.1.201:5442 check port 8009
    server node2-cluster-b 192.168.1.202:5443 check port 8009
    server node3-cluster-b 192.168.1.203:5444 check port 8009

# Cluster B Read
listen pg-cluster-b-read
    bind *:6001
    mode tcp
    balance roundrobin
    option httpchk GET /replica
    http-check expect status 200
    default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2
    server node1-cluster-b 192.168.1.201:5442 check port 8009
    server node2-cluster-b 192.168.1.202:5443 check port 8009
    server node3-cluster-b 192.168.1.203:5444 check port 8009

root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl reload haproxy

# Verify
root@node1:~$ psql -h localhost -p 5000 -U postgres -d postgres -c "SELECT 'Cluster A: Write OK'"
 ?column?
-----------------
 Cluster A: Write OK

root@node1:~$ psql -h localhost -p 6000 -U postgres -d postgres -c "SELECT 'Cluster B: Write OK'"
 ?column?
-----------------
 Cluster B: Write OK

sections.title: Part 10: Monitoring Your HA Cluster
content: Monitoring is essential for any production deployment. Patroni provides multiple ways to monitor your cluster's health.

subsections.title: Patroni REST API Endpoints
subsections.content: Each Patroni node exposes a REST API on port 8008 (or 8009 for Cluster B).

items.label: Useful API endpoints

subsections.title: HAProxy Statistics Dashboard
subsections.content: The HAProxy stats dashboard provides a web-based view of all backends and their health.

items.label: Access HAProxy dashboard
items.code:
# Open in browser:
http://192.168.1.201:7000/stats

# Login:
Username: admin
Password: admin-pass-123

# Or check via command line:
root@node1:~$ curl -u admin:admin-pass-123 http://localhost:7000/stats | grep -E "pg-|UP|DOWN"

items.label: Check replication status
items.code:
postgres=# SELECT client_addr, state, sync_state, sent_lsn, write_lsn, flush_lsn, replay_lsn FROM pg_stat_replication;
  client_addr  |   state   | sync_state |  sent_lsn  |  write_lsn |  flush_lsn | replay_lsn
---------------+---------------+------------+------------+------------+------------+------------
 192.168.1.202 | streaming  | async      | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000
 192.168.1.203 | streaming  | async      | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000  | 0/9500000
(2 rows)

# Check replication lag
postgres=# SELECT pg_size_pretty(pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn)) AS lag FROM pg_stat_replication;
   lag   
---------
 0 bytes
 0 bytes
(2 rows)

sections.title: Part 11: Backup & Point-in-Time Recovery
content: High availability protects against server failures, but you still need backups to protect against data corruption, accidental deletions, and disasters.

subsections.title: Enable WAL Archiving
subsections.content: Configure PostgreSQL to archive WAL files for point-in-time recovery.

items.label: Update Patroni config for WAL archiving
items.code:
# Edit Patroni configuration to add WAL archiving parameters
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml edit-config

# Add these parameters (in interactive editor):
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_mode: 'on'
    archive_command: 'pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha archive-push %p'
    archive_timeout: 60

# Or update via patronictl
root@node1:~$ sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml update-config --apply - <<EOF
postgresql:
  parameters:
    archive_mode: 'on'
    archive_command: 'pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha archive-push %p'
    archive_timeout: 60
EOF

items.label: Install pgBackRest
items.code: root@node1:~$ sudo apt-get install pgbackrest -y

items.label: Configure pgBackRest
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo nano /etc/pgbackrest/pgbackrest.conf

[pg-ha]
pg1-path=/data/patroni/pg
pg1-port=5432
pg1-user=postgres

[global]
repo1-path=/backup/pgbackrest
repo1-retention-full=4
repo1-retention-diff=7
repo1-cipher-type=none
process-max=4
compress-type=gz

[global:archive-push]
compress-level=6

items.label: Create stanza and run first backup
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo mkdir -p /backup/pgbackrest
root@node1:~$ sudo chown -R postgres:postgres /backup
root@node1:~$ sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha stanza-create

# Take a full backup
root@node1:~$ sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=full backup

# Verify backup
root@node1:~$ sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha info
stanza: pg-ha
    status: ok
    cipher: none

    db (current)
        wal archive min/max (16-1): 000000010000000000000001 / 00000001000000000000000A

        full backup: 20260519-103000F
            timestamp start/stop: 2026-05-19 10:30:00 / 2026-05-19 10:35:00
            size: 512MB, backup size: 128MB

items.label: Restore to a specific point in time
items.code:
# Stop the cluster first
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop patroni

# Restore from backup
root@node1:~$ sudo -u postgres pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=time --target="2026-05-19 09:30:00" --target-action=promote restore

# Restart Patroni (it will detect restored data)
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni

items.label: Daily cron for backups
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo crontab -e

# Full backup every Sunday at 2 AM
0 2 * * 0 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=full backup >> /var/log/pgbackrest-full.log 2>&1

# Differential backup every day at 3 AM (except Sunday)
0 3 * * 1-6 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha --type=diff backup >> /var/log/pgbackrest-diff.log 2>&1

# Verify backups weekly
0 4 * * 0 /usr/bin/pgbackrest --stanza=pg-ha check

sections.title: Troubleshooting
sections.description: Common issues you might encounter and how to resolve them.

subsections.title: Split Brain (Multiple Leaders Detected)
subsections.content: In rare cases, network issues might cause two nodes to think they're the leader.

items.label: Symptoms & Solution
text: Symptom: patronictl list shows two nodes in 'Leader' role.
Solution: Stop Patroni on one of the leaders and restart it.
Patroni will use pg_rewind to sync the node as a replica.

items.label: Fix split brain
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl stop patroni
# Check which node has the most recent data
# That one should remain leader
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl start patroni
# The stopped node will auto-join as replica

subsections.title: Patroni Can't Connect to ETCD
subsections.content: If Patroni loses connection to ETCD, it cannot manage the cluster.

items.label: Check ETCD health
items.code:
root@node1:~$ etcdctl endpoint health --cluster

# If ETCD is down, restart it
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl status etcd
root@node1:~$ sudo systemctl restart etcd

# Check Patroni logs
root@node1:~$ sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 50 --no-pager | grep -i error

subsections.title: Replication Lag
subsections.content: High replication lag can cause data loss during failover.

items.label: Check and fix replication lag
items.code:
postgres=# SELECT application_name, state, pg_size_pretty(
    pg_wal_lsn_diff(pg_current_wal_lsn(), replay_lsn)
) AS lag FROM pg_stat_replication;

# Common causes of lag:
# - Slow network between nodes
# - Under-provisioned replicas (CPU/memory)
# - Long-running queries on replicas
# - wal_keep_size too small

# Solutions:
# 1. Increase wal_keep_size
# 2. Add more CPU/RAM to replicas
# 3. Check network latency
# 4. Reduce load on replicas (use connection pooling)

items.label: Diagnose startup failure
items.code:
root@node1:~$ sudo journalctl -u patroni -n 100 --no-pager

# Common issues:
# - Port already in use
# - Permissions on data directory
# - Python dependency missing

# Fix port conflicts
root@node1:~$ netstat -tlnp | grep 5432
root@node1:~$ netstat -tlnp | grep 8008

# Fix permissions
root@node1:~$ sudo chown -R patroni:postgres /data/patroni
root@node1:~$ sudo chmod 700 /data/patroni

# Test configuration
root@node1:~$ sudo -u patroni patroni /etc/patroni/patroni.yml --validate

sections.title: Summary & Best Practices
content: You've built a complete PostgreSQL High Availability cluster with Patroni, ETCD, and HAProxy. Let's recap what you've accomplished and review some best practices.

rows: Distributed key-value store for leader election

items.label: Production Checklist
text: ✓ Minimum 3 nodes for reliable quorum (5 for large deployments)
✓ Separate network for replication traffic (low latency, high bandwidth)
✓ Synchronous replication for zero data loss (set synchronous_mode: true)
✓ Regular failover testing (at least once a month)
✓ Monitor replication lag (alert if > 100MB)
✓ Enable WAL archiving and backup automation
✓ Set up Patroni REST API authentication
✓ Use separate ports for different clusters
✓ Configure alerts for node failures
✓ Document your failover procedures

items.label: Final Architecture Diagram
text: │              APPLICATION LAYER                               │
│    Web Apps • API Services • Microservices                  │
│              HAPROXY LOAD BALANCER                           │
│    Write: Port 5000 → Leader    Read: Port 5001 → Replicas  │
│ Node 1      │    │ Node 2       │    │ Node 3          │
│ PostgreSQL  │    │ PostgreSQL   │    │ PostgreSQL      │
│ Patroni     │    │ Patroni      │    │ Patroni         │
│ ETCD        │    │ ETCD         │    │ ETCD            │
│      ETCD CLUSTER           │
│  Leader Election + Config   │

infoBox.content: Always test your failover procedures in a staging environment before deploying to production. Automate failover tests using tools like Chaos Engineering or LitmusChaos to build confidence in your HA setup.

items.label: Troubleshooting Commands Quick Reference
items.code:
# Quick cluster status
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml list
sudo patronictl -c /etc/patroni/patroni.yml topology

# Check all nodes
curl -s http://node1:8008/health
curl -s http://node2:8008/cluster

# Check ETCD
export ETCDCTL_API=3
sudo etcdctl endpoint health --cluster

# Restart services in order
sudo systemctl restart etcd
sudo systemctl restart patroni
sudo systemctl reload haproxy

# View logs
sudo journalctl -u patroni -f
sudo journalctl -u etcd -n 100
sudo tail -f /var/log/haproxy.log
